draft.introduction: The parable of the Prodigal Son, the most celebrated parable in the Gospels. A son squanders his inheritance, returns in shame, and is met by a father who runs to embrace him—a portrait of divine mercy.

translation.text: And he said, “There was a man who had two sons. And the younger of them said to his father, ‘Father, give me the share of property that is coming to me.’ And he divided his property between them. And he arose and came to his father. But while he was still a long way off, his father saw him and felt compassion, and ran and embraced him and kissed him. And the son said, ‘Father, I have sinned against heaven and before you.’ But the father said to his servants, ‘Bring quickly the best robe and put it on him, for this my son was dead, and is alive again.’”
translation.original_text: ὅτι οὗτος ὁ υἱός μου νεκρὸς ἦν καὶ ἀνέζησεν

translator_notes.note: The father “ran” (δραμών)—a detail that would have shocked ancient audiences. A patriarch running was undignified in Mediterranean culture. The father abandons his honor to meet the son, a deliberate image of God’s willingness to humiliate himself for reconciliation.
